prompt.question: Which one is true in relation to the @Qualifier annotation?
feedbacks:
@Qualifier is NOT sufficient on its own — it must be used together with @Autowired (or @Inject) to trigger dependency injection.

Incorrect example:

@Qualifier("userService")
private UserService service; // Will NOT be injected!

Correct example:

@Autowired
@Qualifier("userService")
private UserService service; // Will be injected

@Qualifier is only a helper annotation that tells Spring which bean to choose when multiple candidates exist. Without @Autowired or @Inject, Spring doesn’t know that injection is required.

When Spring performs autowiring by type and finds multiple candidates, it uses the field name as an implicit qualifier. This is the default behavior in Spring.

Example:

@Component
public class PrimaryEmailService implements EmailService { }

@Component
public class SecondaryEmailService implements EmailService { }

// This will match the bean named 'primaryEmailService'
@Autowired
private EmailService primaryEmailService;

// Or explicitly specify it:
@Autowired
@Qualifier("secondaryEmailService")
private EmailService emailService;

In the first case, even without @Qualifier, Spring matches the field name with the bean name automatically.

This statement is INCORRECT — @Qualifier is not ignored on collections.
It works properly and filters which beans should be injected.

Example:

@Component
@Qualifier("fast")
public class QuickSort implements SortAlgorithm { }

@Component
@Qualifier("slow")
public class BubbleSort implements SortAlgorithm { }

@Component
@Qualifier("fast")
public class MergeSort implements SortAlgorithm { }

// Only beans marked with @Qualifier("fast") will be injected
@Autowired
@Qualifier("fast")
private List<SortAlgorithm> fastAlgorithms; // [QuickSort, MergeSort]

// Without @Qualifier, all beans will be injected
@Autowired
private List<SortAlgorithm> allAlgorithms; // [QuickSort, BubbleSort, MergeSort]

As shown, @Qualifier successfully filters the injected beans.

This statement is INCORRECT.
Both Spring’s @Qualifier and JSR-330’s @Qualifier (from javax.inject) can be used to create custom qualifier annotations — they are interchangeable.

Spring version:

@Target({ElementType.FIELD, ElementType.PARAMETER, ElementType.TYPE})
@Retention(RetentionPolicy.RUNTIME)
@org.springframework.beans.factory.annotation.Qualifier
public @interface FastAlgorithm { }

JSR-330 version:

@Target({ElementType.FIELD, ElementType.PARAMETER, ElementType.TYPE})
@Retention(RetentionPolicy.RUNTIME)
@javax.inject.Qualifier
public @interface Premium { }

Both approaches work perfectly in Spring, as the framework fully supports JSR-330 annotations.
prompt.explanation:
When @Qualifier is specified, the Spring container will autowire the annotated variable, whether @Autowired is specified or not.:
Incorrect, @Qualifier is used in conjunction with @Autowired to further qualify the dependency injection.

The bean name is considered a default qualifier value.:
Correct, when you define a bean, the bean name can be used as a qualifier for autowiring.

@Qualifier annotations applied on collections (e.g. Set) are ignored.:
Incorrect, @Qualifier can be used to specify which beans should be included in the collection.

Custom qualifier annotations are built using Spring’s @Qualifier. JSR 330’s @Qualifier cannot be used.:
Incorrect, custom qualifier annotations can be created using Spring’s @Qualifier, and JSR 330’s @Qualifier can also be used for the same purpose.
answers: @Qualifier alone (without @Autowired) is sufficient to trigger dependency injection.
When multiple candidates exist, Spring uses the field (or parameter) name as an implicit qualifier to match the bean name.
@Qualifier annotations applied on collections (e.g., List, Set) are always ignored and have no effect.
Custom qualifier annotations must use Spring's @Qualifier as a meta-annotation. JSR-330's @Qualifier cannot be used for this purpose.

results.question_plain: Which one is true in relation to the @Qualifier annotation?

prompt.question: In which of these database system isolation levels can non-repeatable reads occur?
feedbacks: In the Serializable isolation level, transactions are executed in a way that ensures full isolation, meaning that no other transactions can access the data that is being modified until the transaction is completed. This level prevents non-repeatable reads from occurring.
In the Repeatable Reads isolation level, once a transaction reads a piece of data, it will not change even if another transaction modifies it. This level prevents non-repeatable reads from occurring.

In the Read Uncommitted isolation level, transactions can read data that has been modified by other transactions but not yet committed. This can lead to non-repeatable reads where the same query may return different results within the same transaction.

In the Read Committed isolation level, transactions can only read data that has been committed by other transactions. However, if a transaction reads the same data multiple times within the same transaction, it may see different values if other transactions have modified the data in between reads. This can lead to non-repeatable reads.

prompt.question: Which of these are true in Spring aspects-oriented programming?
feedbacks:
• This is a fundamental limitation of proxy-based AOP.

  • When a method inside a class calls another method of the same class, the call does not go through the proxy, and thus AOP advice is not applied.

Example:

@Component
public class MyService {
    @Transactional
    public void methodA() {
        methodB(); // AOP advice for methodB() is NOT applied
    }

    @Transactional
    public void methodB() {
        // Transactional advice expected here — but won't run
    }
}

1. JDK dynamic proxies can only intercept public methods defined on interfaces.

  2. They do not work for:

    • Protected methods ❌

    • Methods not declared in an interface ❌

    • Private or package-private methods ❌

• CGLIB proxies are created by subclassing the target class.

  • This allows Spring AOP to intercept public, protected, and even package-private methods.

  • Private methods, however, are not intercepted.

  • So public methods will be intercepted when using CGLIB.

• CGLIB cannot override private methods, so they cannot be intercepted.

  • AOP proxies only apply to methods that are visible to the proxy subclass.
prompt.explanation:
• JDK Dynamic Proxies:

    • Only work with interfaces

    • Can only intercept public methods declared in the interface

  • CGLIB Proxies:

    • Work with classes (even without interfaces)

    • Can intercept public, protected, and package-private methods

    • Cannot intercept private or final methods

  • Self-invocation never goes through the proxy → No interception occurs
answers: Due to the proxy-based nature of Spring’s AOP framework, calls within the target object are by definition not intercepted.
If you are using JDK proxies, only public and protected interface method calls on the proxy can be intercepted.
If you are using CGLIB, public method calls on the proxy will be intercepted.
If you are using CGLIB, private method calls on the proxy will be intercepted.

prompt.question: On which of these database system isolation levels can non-repeatable reads happen?

Correct, non-repeatable reads can occur at this isolation level because data read in one transaction can be modified by another transaction before the first transaction completes.

REPEATABLE_READ:
Incorrect, this isolation level prevents non-repeatable reads by ensuring that if a row is read twice in the same transaction, the same data is returned each time.

SERIALIZABLE:
Incorrect, this is the highest isolation level and prevents non-repeatable reads by ensuring that transactions are completely isolated from one another.

READ_UNCOMMITED:
Correct, this is the lowest isolation level and allows non-repeatable reads, as transactions may see uncommitted changes made by other transactions.

prompt.question: Is the following statement correct? Inversion of Control (IoC) is also known as Dependency Injection (DI).
feedbacks:
Incorrect.

  1. IoC is the broader concept.

  2. DI is one implementation of IoC.

  3. There are other ways to achieve IoC, for example:

    • Service Locator pattern

    • Template Method, Observer and other patterns where the framework calls your code, not the other way around.
prompt.explanation:
Inversion of Control (IoC) vs Dependency Injection (DI)

Inversion of Control (IoC) is a general design principle.
It means: “Don’t let the object create or control its own dependencies – instead, something else will provide them or call it.”

In traditional code, your class is in control:

public class UserService {
    private UserRepository userRepository = new UserRepository(); // creates its own dependency
}

Here, UserService decides which UserRepository to use and how to create it. The control is inside the class.

With Inversion of Control, this responsibility is inverted: the framework (like Spring) or an external component takes control over object creation and wiring. Your class just declares what it needs.

Dependency Injection (DI)

Dependency Injection (DI) is one concrete technique to implement IoC.

Instead of creating dependencies inside the class, we inject them from the outside, for example via constructor:

@Service
public class UserService {

    private final UserRepository userRepository;

    // Dependency is injected from outside
    public UserService(UserRepository userRepository) {
        this.userRepository = userRepository;
    }
}

In Spring:

  • The IoC container (a.k.a. Spring container) creates objects (beans),

  • Decides their lifecycle,

  • And injects the dependencies (e.g. UserRepository into UserService).

So:

  • IoC = idea: “the framework controls the flow and object creation, not your classes”.

  • DI = mechanism: “give the class its dependencies from outside (constructor, setter, field) instead of creating them inside”.

prompt.question: Which of the following limitations are present in both proxy-types(JDK Dynamic proxy and CGLIB proxy) supported by Spring?
feedbacks:
• Self-invocation (calling a method from another method inside the same class) bypasses the proxy in both JDK dynamic and CGLIB proxies.

  • This means that Spring AOP advice won't be applied when you call a proxied method from another method within the same bean instance.

Example (Problematic):

@Component
public class MyService {

    @Transactional
    public void methodA() {
        methodB(); // This call bypasses the proxy!
    }

    @Transactional
    public void methodB() {
        // Transaction advice is not applied
    }
}

• This applies only to CGLIB proxies, because CGLIB works by subclassing your class and overriding methods.

  • Final methods cannot be overridden, so CGLIB cannot advise them.

  • JDK dynamic proxies are interface-based, and don’t care about final modifiers — they only work on public interface methods.

Again, this applies only to CGLIB:

  • Since it uses subclassing, if a class is final, CGLIB cannot create a proxy.

JDK dynamic proxies don't proxy classes at all — they proxy interfaces. So this limitation does not apply to JDK proxies.

Neither proxy type can intercept private methods, because proxies (whether via subclassing or interfaces) cannot call or override private methods.

Spring AOP only supports public (and sometimes protected/package-private) method execution join points.
answers: Both do not work for final methods
Both do not work for final classes
Both do not work for private methods

prompt.question: What are the advantages of an application that is built making use of dependency injection?
feedbacks: Dependency injection allows for high cohesion, high readability, and low coupling in an application. By injecting dependencies from external sources, the application components are more focused on their specific tasks, leading to better organization and maintainability.

High cohesion is a benefit of dependency injection, but it is not the only advantage. Dependency injection helps in keeping related functionality together, but it also promotes reusability, testability, and flexibility in the application design.

High readability is another advantage of dependency injection, as it makes the code easier to understand and follow. By clearly defining dependencies and their injection points, developers can quickly grasp the flow of the application and make changes more confidently.

Low coupling is a key advantage of using dependency injection in an application. By decoupling components and their dependencies, the application becomes more flexible, easier to maintain, and less prone to cascading changes when modifications are made. This leads to a more robust and scalable software architecture.
